'''Darren Smith''' (born 8 December 1968) is an Australian former [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s. He played for the [[Australian national rugby league team|Australian]] national side and also [[Queensland State of Origin Team|Queensland]] in the [[Rugby League State of Origin|State of Origin]]. His club football career was spent with the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]], [[Brisbane Broncos]]<ref name=profile>{{Cite web |url=http://www.stats.rleague.com/rl/scorers/players/Darren_Smith.html |title=Darren Smith |access-date=18 February 2010 |archive-date=10 October 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091010222425/http://stats.rleague.com/rl/scorers/players/Darren_Smith.html |url-status=dead }}</ref> and [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. He is the brother of fellow Maroon and international, [[Jason Smith (rugby league)|Jason Smith]].

==Background==
Born in [[Brisbane]], [[Queensland]] on 8 December 1968, Darren Smith went on to play in the [[Brisbane Rugby League premiership]]. During the [[1988 Great Britain Lions tour]] of Australia, Smith was selected at 19 years of age to play for the [[Brisbane rugby league team]] against the visiting British.

==Playing career==

===1990s===
In 1990 he moved from Brisbane's [[Easts Tigers]] to Sydney's [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]].<ref name=profile/> Conspicuous in headgear after suffering a severe concussion in 1991, Smith made his [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] début as a reserve for [[Queensland rugby league team|Queensland]] in the [[1992 State of Origin series|1992 series]]. During the [[1993 State of Origin series]] Smith played from the interchange bench n Games 2 and 3, both losses.

Smith was selected to play on the interchange bench in all three games of the [[1994 State of Origin series]] which was eventually lost by Queensland. He looked certain to press for Australian selection until off-field politics took its toll in the [[1994 NSWRL season]]. Smith's refusal to renew his contract with Canterbury, negotiating with two Brisbane clubs instead, saw him used in the limited role of interchange player. Smith's {{rlp|ce}} place in Canterbury's [[1994 NSWRL season|grand final]] side was taken by youngster [[Steven Hughes (rugby league)|Steven Hughes]] who made little impact when brought on to the field as a replacement.  Smith revealed that he had signed with the [[South Queensland Crushers]] but he then did an about-face and successfully sought a release to play with the [[Brisbane Broncos 1995|Brisbane Broncos in 1995]].

It was unfortunate that Smith hit possibly his best form at a time when the code was in turmoil, and he did not represent again until 1997's [[Super League (Australia) season 1997|Super League season]].  He proved his durability at the highest level, making his Test début against [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] and playing at lock forward in the [[1997 Anzac Test]], the first [[Anzac Test]], as well as all five Tests against the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] and [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] as well as making three appearances in Queensland's [[Super League (Australia)#Tri-series|Tri-Series]] side.

When the game reunified for the [[1998 NRL season]], Smith returned to the Queensland State of Origin team and was the [[National Rugby League]]'s top try-scorer for the season, crossing for the final try in Brisbane's 38–12 thrashing of his former club Canterbury in the [[1998 NRL grand final]]. In an ironic twist to his career, Smith announced that he was returning to the Bulldogs in [[National Rugby League season 1999|1999]].<ref name=profile/>
Smith was selected for the [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian team]] to play in the [[1999 ANZAC Test]], and to compete in the end of season [[1999 Tri Nations]] tournament. In the final against [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] he played at centre in the Kangaroos' 22–20 victory.

===2000s===
In 2000 he was awarded the [[Australian Sports Medal]] for his contribution to Australia's international standing in rugby league. Smith left the Bulldogs at the end of [[National Rugby League season 2002|2002]] (denied the opportunity to finally win a premiership with the club when it was stripped of its competition points) to play with English club [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. Having won [[Super League VI]], St Helens contested the [[2003 World Club Challenge]] against 2002 NRL Premiers, the [[Sydney Roosters]]. Smith played at second-row forward in Saints' 38–0 loss. At the end of his first English season Smith was unexpectedly called into the Third Test of the [[Rugby League Ashes|Ashes series]] (despite being 34 years old and outside the NRL) by his former Bulldogs coach, national coach [[Chris Anderson (rugby league)|Chris Anderson]], who felt the available [[2003 Kangaroo tour|2003 Kangaroo]] {{rlp|ce}}s were not experienced enough.

==Retirement==
Smith returned to Australia in 2004 and made cameo appearances with the Broncos in two matches while playing for his former club, [[Eastern Suburbs Tigers]]. At age 36 years and 284 days, he was the NRL's oldest player in 2005. Smith finally retired as a player after Easts were beaten in the grand final. At the time of his retirement Smith held the record for [[Brisbane Broncos Records#Most tries in a season|most tries in a season for the Broncos]] (together with [[Steve Renouf]]) at 23.
